title: For Participants
text:
A real piece of code you’re working on. It doesn’t need to be:

Finished
Working correctly
Well-organised
In a language your partner knows

If you’re not sure what to bring, pick a script you run regularly. There’s almost always something to improve, explain, or make clearer.

title: For Participants
text:
A real piece of code you’re working on. It doesn’t need to be:

Finished
Working correctly
Well-organised
In a language your partner knows

If you’re not sure what to bring, pick a script you run regularly. There’s almost always something to improve, explain, or make clearer.

title: For Participants
text:
The two roles
Driver — controls the keyboard and types. Focuses on the immediate task: writing, reading, and editing code.
Navigator — watches, thinks ahead, and asks questions. Focuses on the bigger picture: does this approach make sense, are there edge cases, what should happen next?
Switch roles regularly — every 10–15 minutes, or when you reach a natural pause.

title: For Participants
text:
Before you start each round

Give your partner a 2-minute tour of your code: what does it do, what are you working on, where are you stuck?
Agree who drives first

title: For Participants
text:
While you’re pairing


If you’re the driver

Talk through what you’re doing as you type
Ask the navigator before making big changes
Don’t feel you have to type quickly — thinking out loud matters more


If you’re the navigator

Ask questions freely — “what does this variable hold?”
Don’t grab the keyboard; say what you’d try and let the driver type it
Watch for typos and logic errors the driver might miss



Both of you

It’s fine not to solve the problem — the conversation is the point
Messy, unfinished code is normal and expected; no judgement
If you’re stuck, say so — “I have no idea what this does” is a useful thing to say

title: For Participants
section: Things that often come up in sessions
text:
Things that often come up in sessions

A tool or library your partner uses that you haven’t seen before
A different way of approaching a problem you’ve been stuck on
Something you’ve been doing for years that turns out to have a neater solution
A bug that you spotted the moment you had to explain the code out loud

title: Pair Programming: Participant Reference
text:
Driver — controls the keyboard and types. Focuses on the immediate task: writing, reading, and editing code.
Navigator — watches, thinks ahead, and asks questions. Focuses on the bigger picture: does this approach make sense, are there edge cases, what should happen next?
Switch roles regularly — every 10–15 minutes, or when you reach a natural pause.




Give your partner a 2-minute tour of your code: what does it do, what are you working on, where are you stuck?
Agree who drives first




If you’re the driver - Talk through what you’re doing as you type — “I’m looping over the samples here because…” - Ask the navigator before making big changes — “should I refactor this function or keep going?”
If you’re the navigator - Ask questions freely — “what does this variable hold?” or “why does this need to be a list?” - Don’t grab the keyboard; instead say what you’d try and let the driver type it - Watch for typos and logic errors the driver might miss
Both of you - It’s fine not to solve the problem — the conversation is the point - Messy, unfinished code is normal and expected; no judgement - If you’re stuck, say so — “I have no idea what this does” is a useful thing to say



A real piece of code you’re working on. It doesn’t need to: - Be finished - Work correctly - Be well-organised - Be in a language your partner knows
If you’re not sure what to bring, pick a script you run regularly. There’s almost always something to improve, explain, or make clearer.




A tool or library your partner uses that you haven’t seen before
A different way of approaching a problem you’ve been stuck on
Something you’ve been doing for years that turns out to have a neater solution
A bug that you spotted the moment you had to explain the code out loud

If you’re not sure what to bring, pick a script you run regularly. There’s almost always something to improve, explain, or make clearer.

title: Pair Programming
text:
Why we’re here
Researchers who code often:

Learned by trial and error, or by Googling
Work alone on their code
Have no obvious way to get feedback or learn from peers

This workshop is about changing that — without requiring formal training or preparation.

title: Pair Programming
text:
What is pair programming?
Two people. One computer. One piece of code.

One person drives — they type
One person navigates — they think, question, and guide
You switch roles regularly

title: Pair Programming
text:
The driver



Controls the keyboard. Focuses on the immediate task.




Type and talk at the same time — “I’m looping over samples here because…”
Ask the navigator before making big changes
Don’t feel you have to move quickly

title: Pair Programming
text:
The navigator



Watches, thinks ahead, and asks questions.




Ask freely — “what does this variable hold?”
Don’t grab the keyboard — say what you’d try, let the driver type it
Watch for errors the driver might miss while typing

title: Pair Programming
text:
What to bring
A piece of code you’re working on.
It does not need to be:

Finished
Working
Tidy
In a language your partner knows

If you’re unsure: bring a script you run regularly.

title: Pair Programming
text:
What makes a good session

Talk out loud — the conversation is the value, not the output
Ask freely — “I don’t understand this” is useful, not embarrassing
No judgement — messy code is expected and normal
It’s fine not to solve anything — you’ll still learn something

title: Pair Programming
text:
Today’s format



Time
Activity




Now
This talk (~20 min)


+20 min
Round 1 — pair A’s code (~25 min)


+45 min
Break + new partners (~5 min)


+50 min
Round 2 — pair B’s code (~25 min)


+75 min
Optional round 3 or open hacking


+105 min
Debrief together

title: Pair Programming
text:
Let’s get started
Your pairings for round 1:



(Facilitator: reveal or read out pairings here)



Before you start: give your partner a 2-minute tour of your code — what it does, what you’re working on, where you’re stuck.
Decide who drives first. Timer starts now.

title: For Facilitators
text:
Logistics

Book a room with tables big enough for two people to sit side by side at one laptop
Aim for 6–16 participants; pairs of 2 work best, so keep numbers even if possible
Prepare a random pairing list in advance (or use a random name picker on the day)

What to ask participants to bring

A laptop
A piece of code they’re actively working on — doesn’t need to be finished or working
No other preparation — the whole point is to work on real problems

title: For Facilitators
text:
Logistics

Book a room with tables big enough for two people to sit side by side at one laptop
Aim for 6–16 participants; pairs of 2 work best, so keep numbers even if possible
Prepare a random pairing list in advance (or use a random name picker on the day)

What to ask participants to bring

A laptop
A piece of code they’re actively working on — doesn’t need to be finished or working
No other preparation — the whole point is to work on real problems

title: For Facilitators
text:
The intro talk (~20 minutes)
Suggested structure:

Why we’re here (5 min) — researchers who code often work in isolation; pair programming is a way to learn from peers without needing formal training
How pair programming works (5 min) — explain driver/navigator roles; emphasise that switching regularly is the key mechanic
What makes a good session (5 min) — talk out loud, ask questions freely, no judgement on messy code
What to expect today (5 min) — walk through the format, answer questions







Tip



The slide deck covers all of this — you can use it as-is or adapt it.


Key points to land:

The navigator’s job is to ask questions and think ahead, not to watch silently
“I don’t understand what this does” is a useful thing to say, not an embarrassing one
It’s fine if you don’t solve anything — the conversation is the value

title: For Facilitators
text:
Running the rounds
Pairing

Reveal pairings at the start of each round
Mix experience levels where possible — a more experienced coder paired with a less experienced one usually works well in both directions
In round 2, try to pair people who haven’t worked together yet

Starting a round

Remind pairs to decide who drives first
Suggest the owner of the code gives a 2-minute tour of what it does and what they’re stuck on
Set a visible timer

Mid-round

Walk the room quietly; you don’t need to intervene unless a pair looks stuck or silent
If a pair finishes their problem early, prompt them to switch roles and keep going, or start on the second person’s code early

Ending a round

Give a 2-minute warning
Ask pairs to reach a stopping point rather than stopping mid-thought

title: For Facilitators
text:
Debrief (~15 minutes)
Bring the group back together. Useful prompts:

What did you learn that surprised you?
Did you see a tool or technique you hadn’t heard of?
What would make the next session more useful?

Collect this feedback — it’s useful for iterating the format and for SSI reporting.

title: For Facilitators
text:
Common issues







Issue
Response




“I don’t have any code to bring”
Suggest a script they use regularly — there’s almost always something to improve


Very mismatched skill levels
Usually fine; less experienced people ask good questions, more experienced people learn by explaining


Pairs going quiet
Prompt the navigator: “try asking them to walk you through what this line does”


Code that won’t run on the navigator’s machine
That’s fine — the driver controls the keyboard, navigator looks at the screen

title: Facilitator Guide
text:
Logistics - Book a room with tables big enough for two people to sit side by side at one laptop - Ask participants to bring their laptops with a piece of code they’re actively working on — doesn’t need to be finished or even working - Aim for 6–16 participants; pairs of 2 work best, so keep numbers even if possible - Prepare a random pairing list in advance (or use a random name picker on the day)
What participants should bring - A laptop - A piece of code they’d like to improve, debug, or understand better - No special preparation beyond that — the whole point is to work on real problems



This sets up the mental model participants need. Suggested structure:

Why we’re here (5 min) — researchers who code often work in isolation; pair programming is a way to learn from peers without needing formal training
How pair programming works (5 min) — explain driver/navigator roles; emphasise that switching regularly is the key mechanic
What makes a good session (5 min) — talk out loud, ask questions freely, no judgement on messy code
What to expect today (5 min) — walk through the format, answer questions

Key points to land: - The navigator’s job is to ask questions and think ahead, not to watch silently - “I don’t understand what this does” is a useful thing to say, not an embarrassing one - It’s fine if you don’t solve anything in 25 minutes — the conversation is the value



Pairing - Reveal pairings at the start of each round - Mix experience levels where possible — a more experienced coder paired with a less experienced one usually works well in both directions - In round 2, try to pair people who haven’t worked together yet
Starting a round - Remind pairs to decide who drives first - Suggest the owner of the code gives a 2-minute tour of what it does and what they’re stuck on - Set a visible timer
Mid-round - Walk the room quietly; you don’t need to intervene unless a pair looks stuck or silent - If a pair finishes their problem early, prompt them to switch driver/navigator and keep going, or start on the second person’s code early
Ending a round - Give a 2-minute warning before the timer - Ask pairs to reach a stopping point rather than stopping mid-thought



Bring the group back together and run a brief retrospective. Useful prompts: - What did you learn that surprised you? - Did you see a tool or technique you hadn’t heard of? - What would make the next session more useful?
Collect this feedback — it’s useful for iterating the format and for SSI reporting.



“I don’t have any code to bring” — suggest they bring a script they use regularly, even if it works fine; there’s usually something to improve or explain
Very mismatched skill levels — usually fine; the less experienced person asks good questions, the more experienced person learns by explaining
Pairs going quiet — sometimes people need a prompt; suggest the navigator ask “walk me through what this line does”
Code that won’t run on the navigator’s machine — that’s fine; the driver controls the keyboard, the navigator looks at the screen

title: Facilitator Guide
text:
Logistics - Book a room with tables big enough for two people to sit side by side at one laptop - Ask participants to bring their laptops with a piece of code they’re actively working on — doesn’t need to be finished or even working - Aim for 6–16 participants; pairs of 2 work best, so keep numbers even if possible - Prepare a random pairing list in advance (or use a random name picker on the day)
What participants should bring - A laptop - A piece of code they’d like to improve, debug, or understand better - No special preparation beyond that — the whole point is to work on real problems

Pairing - Reveal pairings at the start of each round - Mix experience levels where possible — a more experienced coder paired with a less experienced one usually works well in both directions - In round 2, try to pair people who haven’t worked together yet
Starting a round - Remind pairs to decide who drives first - Suggest the owner of the code gives a 2-minute tour of what it does and what they’re stuck on - Set a visible timer
Mid-round - Walk the room quietly; you don’t need to intervene unless a pair looks stuck or silent - If a pair finishes their problem early, prompt them to switch driver/navigator and keep going, or start on the second person’s code early
Ending a round - Give a 2-minute warning before the timer - Ask pairs to reach a stopping point rather than stopping mid-thought

title: Facilitator Guide
text:
Bring the group back together and run a brief retrospective. Useful prompts: - What did you learn that surprised you? - Did you see a tool or technique you hadn’t heard of? - What would make the next session more useful?
Collect this feedback — it’s useful for iterating the format and for SSI reporting.

title: Facilitator Guide
text:
“I don’t have any code to bring” — suggest they bring a script they use regularly, even if it works fine; there’s usually something to improve or explain
Very mismatched skill levels — usually fine; the less experienced person asks good questions, the more experienced person learns by explaining
Pairs going quiet — sometimes people need a prompt; suggest the navigator ask “walk me through what this line does”
Code that won’t run on the navigator’s machine — that’s fine; the driver controls the keyboard, the navigator looks at the screen

title: Pair Programming Workshop
text: Many researchers — especially bioinformaticians — write code as part of their work but have had little formal software training. This workshop uses pair programming as a low-barrier way to share skills, build confidence, and learn from peers.

title: Pair Programming Workshop
text:
What is pair programming?
Two people work on one piece of code together, at one computer. One person types (the driver); the other thinks about direction and catches issues (the navigator). They switch regularly.
Unlike code review, you don’t need to understand someone else’s code before you arrive — you bring your own work, so you’re immediately useful to your partner.

title: Pair Programming Workshop
text:
Why it works for researchers

No preparation needed — bring a script you’re working on, finished or not
Real knowledge transfer — you learn by doing, not watching
Cross-pollination — a computational biologist and a genomics analyst may use completely different tools; working together surfaces things neither knew about
Low stakes — messy, half-finished code is expected and welcome

title: Pair Programming Workshop
text:
Event format



Time
Activity




0:00
Welcome and intro talk (~20 min)


0:20
Round 1 — pair A works on person 1’s code (~25 min)


0:45
Short break and partner swap (~5 min)


0:50
Round 2 — pair B works on person 2’s code (~25 min)


1:15
Optional round 3 or open hacking (~30 min)


1:45
Debrief and close (~15 min)



Participants are paired randomly, and pairings rotate each round.

title: Pair Programming Workshop
text:
Get involved

These materials are part of an SSI Fellowship project exploring peer learning for research software developers.
